params.text:
Managing severe bleeding

Heavy bleeding needs to be controlled quickly. If you have them, put on protective gloves to reduce the risk of infection. Then:

  • Apply pressure: press firmly on the wound with a sterile dressing or a clean, non-fluffy pad. If an object is embedded, press around it rather than on it — never pull it out.
  • Dress the wound: secure the dressing with a bandage, firm enough to keep pressure on but not so tight it cuts off circulation.
  • Support and reassure: help the casualty sit or lie down and support the injured part.

Call 999 or 112. If blood soaks through, add another dressing on top and keep up the pressure.

Recognising shock

Serious blood loss (or other causes) can lead to shock — a life-threatening state where not enough oxygen reaches the vital organs. Watch for pale, cold, clammy skin, a fast but weak pulse, rapid shallow breathing, and the casualty feeling faint, dizzy, sick or anxious.

To help: lay them down, raise and support their legs above the level of the heart, loosen tight clothing, keep them warm with a blanket, and reassure them. Do not give food or drink. Call 999 and monitor their breathing, ready to start CPR if they become unresponsive.

params.question: If an object such as a shard of glass is embedded in a bleeding wound, you should pull it out and then press on the wound.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Never remove an embedded object — pulling it out can cause worse bleeding. Press around it instead.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Leave an embedded object in place — it may be plugging the wound. Apply pressure around it and get medical help.
